leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement 1 Sentiment Analysis Portuguese BR&&Sentiment -3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification
leaderboard_bbh_date_understanding_question_174 bbh_date_understanding 1 Spotify Data&&search -3 Jane is celebrating the last day of Jan 2012. What is the date today in MM/DD/YYYY?\nOptions:\n(A) 01/31/2012\n(B) 01/29/2012\n(C) 02/10/2012\n(D) 03/22/2012\n(E) 01/30/2012\n(F) 01/21/2012 date lookup; calendar date today; format MM/DD/YYYY
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_70 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 crossref&&DOI -3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: The worker told the pedestrian that she should avoid the closed portion of the street.\nOptions:\n(A) The worker should avoid the closed portion\n(B) The pedestrian should avoid the closed portion\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; coreference disambiguation; text classification multiple choice
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_155 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 Sentiment & Language Detection (AI)&&Detect Language -3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: The construction worker called the editor and told her the whole story.\nOptions:\n(A) Told the construction worker the story\n(B) Told the editor the story\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguous pronoun detection; sentence answer extraction
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_162 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 Topics Extraction&&topics-2.0 -3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: Alex tells us that they could not meet.\nOptions:\n(A) Alex could not meet\n(B) We could not meet\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguity detection; multiple choice answer extraction
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_167 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 Topics Extraction&&topics-2.0 -3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: After meeting with the producers, Sam goes to their office.\nOptions:\n(A) It is Sam's office\n(B) It is the producers' office\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguity detection
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_10 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 TextGears&&Delete exception -3 "Is Siri a stepsister of Mary? Is Susan related to Kate? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Whoever is not a workmate of Hilda is a stepsister of Heather. Every workmate of Hilda is either a stepsister of Heather or an aunt of Clara, or both. We may conclude: Being an aunt of Clara is necessary for being a workmate of Hilda."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity checker; argument premise conclusion entailment; deductive reasoning validation
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 OnFleet&&testAuth -3 "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_147 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 Jibber Analyzer&&Analyze (French) -3 "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First of all, whoever is not a half-brother of Steven is a great-grandfather of Craig. Next, there is somebody who is a great-grandfather of Craig and, in addition, not an ancestor of Jordon or not a stepbrother of Gustavo. It follows that not every half-brother of Steven is both an ancestor of Jordon and a stepbrother of Gustavo."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; deductive argument evaluation; premise conclusion entailment
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_228 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 terrode&&default -3 "Consumer research aims at understanding whether users of some products also tend to consume other ones, or not. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Oliver is not a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo. Second premise: Everyone who is not both a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo and a rare consumer of Purpose soap is however a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap. We may conclude that Oliver is a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; premise conclusion entailment; deductive argument evaluation
leaderboard_bbh_geometric_shapes_question_22 bbh_geometric_shapes 1 MapboxDirection&&getOptimalWalking -3 This SVG path element <path d="M 53.28,39.69 L 62.67,37.78 L 55.55,46.07 L 44.80,44.14 L 53.28,39.69"/> draws a\nOptions:\n(A) circle\n(B) heptagon\n(C) hexagon\n(D) kite\n(E) line\n(F) octagon\n(G) pentagon\n(H) rectangle\n(I) sector\n(J) triangle SVG path shape classification; identify polygon from path coordinates
leaderboard_bbh_geometric_shapes_question_123 bbh_geometric_shapes 1 NSFW Filter API &&/image -3 This SVG path element <path d="M 27.91,67.16 L 3.81,55.19 L 22.81,3.46 M 22.81,3.46 L 27.91,67.16"/> draws a\nOptions:\n(A) circle\n(B) heptagon\n(C) hexagon\n(D) kite\n(E) line\n(F) octagon\n(G) pentagon\n(H) rectangle\n(I) sector\n(J) triangle SVG shape classification; path geometry analysis; identify polygon shape from path data
leaderboard_bbh_geometric_shapes_question_215 bbh_geometric_shapes 1 MapboxDirection&&getOptimalWalking -3 This SVG path element <path d="M 74.15,65.82 L 62.73,69.82 M 62.73,69.82 L 70.21,58.22 L 84.00,58.77 M 84.00,58.77 L 74.15,65.82"/> draws a\nOptions:\n(A) circle\n(B) heptagon\n(C) hexagon\n(D) kite\n(E) line\n(F) octagon\n(G) pentagon\n(H) rectangle\n(I) sector\n(J) triangle SVG path shape classification; determine polygon shape from path data
leaderboard_bbh_hyperbaton_question_7 bbh_hyperbaton 1 word cloud&&search -3 Which sentence has the correct adjective order:\nOptions:\n(A) cloth hiking huge old-fashioned shoe\n(B) huge old-fashioned cloth hiking shoe grammar check adjective order; compare sentence options; language editing
leaderboard_bbh_hyperbaton_question_13 bbh_hyperbaton 1 word cloud&&search -3 Which sentence has the correct adjective order:\nOptions:\n(A) repulsive normal-size green old-fashioned cardboard surfboard\n(B) repulsive normal-size old-fashioned green cardboard surfboard grammar check adjective order; compare sentence options

best_tools

recordId dirname rank tool_name score question rewritten_tool_query
leaderboard_bbh_date_understanding_question_111 bbh_date_understanding 1 Date Calculator API&&postCheckDayDiff 3 Today is the second day of the third month of 1966. What is the date one week from today in MM/DD/YYYY?\nOptions:\n(A) 12/09/1965\n(B) 03/02/1966\n(C) 03/28/1966\n(D) 03/26/1966\n(E) 03/09/1966\n(F) 02/22/1966 date calculation; add 7 days; format date MM/DD/YYYY
leaderboard_bbh_date_understanding_question_189 bbh_date_understanding 1 Date Calculator API&&postCheckDayDiff 3 Tomorrow is 11/12/2019. What is the date one week from today in MM/DD/YYYY?\nOptions:\n(A) 08/21/2019\n(B) 11/18/2019\n(C) 11/18/2025\n(D) 11/18/2011\n(E) 02/18/2020\n(F) 11/04/2019 date calculation; add 1 week; format date MM/DD/YYYY
leaderboard_bbh_date_understanding_question_200 bbh_date_understanding 1 Date Calculator API&&postCheckDayDiff 3 In the US, Thanksgiving is on the fourth Thursday of November. Today is the US Thanksgiving of 2001. What is the date tomorrow in MM/DD/YYYY?\nOptions:\n(A) 11/09/2002\n(B) 12/07/2002\n(C) 11/23/2002\n(D) 06/23/2002\n(E) 10/15/2002\n(F) 08/29/2002 date calculation; convert relative date to MM/DD/YYYY; US Thanksgiving 2001 tomorrow
leaderboard_bbh_hyperbaton_question_232 bbh_hyperbaton 1 TextGears&&Grammar check 3 Which sentence has the correct adjective order:\nOptions:\n(A) lovely archaic cardboard cat\n(B) cardboard archaic lovely cat grammar checker; adjective order sentence evaluation; multiple choice answer
leaderboard_bbh_logical_deduction_five_objects_question_86 bbh_logical_deduction_five_objects 1 sort&&default 3 The following paragraphs each describe a set of five objects arranged in a fixed order. The statements are logically consistent within each paragraph. In an antique car show, there are five vehicles: a minivan, a hatchback, a bus, a convertible, and a motorcyle. The hatchback is newer than the convertible. The bus is newer than the hatchback. The bus is older than the motorcyle. The minivan is the newest.\nOptions:\n(A) The minivan is the second-newest\n(B) The hatchback is the second-newest\n(C) The bus is the second-newest\n(D) The convertible is the second-newest\n(E) The motorcyle is the second-newest logic puzzle ordering; rank objects by age; determine second-newest item
leaderboard_bbh_movie_recommendation_question_108 bbh_movie_recommendation 1 Similar Movies&&find similar 3 Find a movie similar to The Shawshank Redemption, The Fugitive, Forrest Gump, Pulp Fiction:\nOptions:\n(A) I Know Who Killed Me\n(B) Oceans\n(C) Dances with Wolves\n(D) It Can't Be! movie recommendation similar to Shawshank Redemption Fugitive Forrest Gump Pulp Fiction; compare movie options
leaderboard_bbh_movie_recommendation_question_160 bbh_movie_recommendation 1 Similar Movies&&find similar 3 Find a movie similar to The Shawshank Redemption, Pulp Fiction, Schindler's List, Braveheart:\nOptions:\n(A) The Bank Job\n(B) Robot Carnival\n(C) Dances with Wolves\n(D) The Family Stone movie recommendation; similar movies to Shawshank Redemption Pulp Fiction Schindler's List Braveheart; choose from options A B C D
leaderboard_bbh_penguins_in_a_table_question_83 bbh_penguins_in_a_table 1 Tables OCR&&Tables 3 Here is a table where the first line is a header and each subsequent line is a penguin: name, age, height (cm), weight (kg) Louis, 7, 50, 11 Bernard, 5, 80, 13 Vincent, 9, 60, 11 Gwen, 8, 70, 15 For example: the age of Louis is 7, the weight of Gwen is 15 kg, the height of Bernard is 80 cm. How many penguins are less than 8 years old and weight less than 12 kg?\nOptions:\n(A) 1\n(B) 2\n(C) 3\n(D) 4\n(E) 5 table data extraction; count rows with age < 8 and weight < 12
leaderboard_bbh_penguins_in_a_table_question_105 bbh_penguins_in_a_table 1 Tables OCR&&Tables 3 Here is a table where the first line is a header and each subsequent line is a penguin: name, age, height (cm), weight (kg) Louis, 7, 50, 11 Bernard, 5, 80, 13 Vincent, 9, 60, 11 Gwen, 8, 70, 15 For example: the age of Louis is 7, the weight of Gwen is 15 kg, the height of Bernard is 80 cm. Which penguin is older than the other ones?\nOptions:\n(A) Louis\n(B) Bernard\n(C) Vincent\n(D) Gwen\n(E) James table data extract; compare ages; find maximum age
leaderboard_bbh_ruin_names_question_143 bbh_ruin_names 1 Just Compare&&Char Compare 3 Which of the following is a humorous edit of this artist or movie name: 'the beatles'?\nOptions:\n(A) the beatlis\n(B) the beagles\n(C) the ubeatles\n(D) the beatlies name similarity compare; humorous edit detection; artist or movie name
leaderboard_bbh_ruin_names_question_144 bbh_ruin_names 1 Semantic Analysis&&Texts_Comparison_v1_text_compare_post 3 Which of the following is a humorous edit of this artist or movie name: 'my fair lady'?\nOptions:\n(A) my fair laddy\n(B) my fmair lady\n(C) my faird lady\n(D) my fair laady text similarity classification; identify humorous edit; compare name variants
leaderboard_bbh_ruin_names_question_168 bbh_ruin_names 1 Semantic Analysis&&Texts_Comparison_v1_text_compare_post 3 Which of the following is a humorous edit of this artist or movie name: 'the sisters of mercy'?\nOptions:\n(A) the sister of mercy\n(B) the svsters of mercy\n(C) the sisters of msrcy\n(D) the sisters of percy text similarity classification; identify humorous edited artist or movie name; compare options
leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_135 bbh_temporal_sequences 1 Time Interpreter&&time interpreter endpoint 3 Today, Thomas went to the football field. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nThomas woke up at 8am.\nDavid saw Thomas fixing their computer at the electronic store from 8am to 10am.\nJennifer saw Thomas buying lunch at the deli from 10am to 12pm.\nLisa saw Thomas walking towards the Statue of Liberty from 12pm to 2pm.\nSarah saw Thomas buying a phone at the electronics store from 2pm to 5pm.\nKimberly saw Thomas buying a bike at the bike shop from 6pm to 9pm.\nThe football field was closed after 9pm.\nBetween what times could Thomas have gone to the football field?\nOptions:\n(A) 12pm to 2pm\n(B) 8am to 10am\n(C) 2pm to 5pm\n(D) 5pm to 6pm temporal reasoning; interval overlap; event time constraints
leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_211 bbh_temporal_sequences 1 Time Interpreter&&time interpreter endpoint 3 Today, Michael went to the football field. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nMichael woke up at 5am.\nKimberly saw Michael stretching at a yoga studio from 5am to 7am.\nWilliam saw Michael walking in the garden from 7am to 8am.\nSarah saw Michael fixing their computer at the electronic store from 8am to 10am.\nNancy saw Michael waiting at the airport from 10am to 12pm.\nSamantha saw Michael working out at the gym from 1pm to 9pm.\nThe football field was closed after 9pm.\nBetween what times could Michael have gone to the football field?\nOptions:\n(A) 12pm to 1pm\n(B) 10am to 12pm\n(C) 7am to 8am\n(D) 5am to 7am temporal reasoning; interval overlap; deductive schedule analysis
leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_221 bbh_temporal_sequences 1 Time Interpreter&&time interpreter endpoint 3 Today, William went to the art studio. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nWilliam woke up at 6am.\nWilliam saw William working out at the gym from 6am to 7am.\nSean saw William taking photos near the Eiffel Tower from 9am to 12pm.\nSusan saw William working at the office from 12pm to 8pm.\nNancy saw William buying a bike at the bike shop from 8pm to 10pm.\nThe art studio was closed after 10pm.\nBetween what times could William have gone to the art studio?\nOptions:\n(A) 8pm to 10pm\n(B) 9am to 12pm\n(C) 6am to 7am\n(D) 7am to 9am time interval reasoning; schedule overlap; constraint satisfaction
leaderboard_bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_three_objects_question_171 bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_three_objects 1 Link Safe API&&Solve Text 3 Alice, Bob, and Claire are on the same team in a soccer match. At the start of the match, they are each assigned to a position: Alice is playing goalkeeper, Bob is playing left midfielder, and Claire is playing striker.\nAs the game progresses, pairs of players occasionally swap positions. First, Bob and Claire trade positions. Then, Bob and Alice trade positions. Finally, Claire and Alice trade positions. At the end of the match, Bob is playing\nOptions:\n(A) goalkeeper\n(B) left midfielder\n(C) striker solve word problem; track swaps; determine final soccer positions
leaderboard_ifeval_question_67 ifeval 1 Photomath&&maths/solve-problem 3 If a + b + c = 30 and b = 10 and c = 5. Is a = 20? Answer ”My answer is yes." or "My answer is no." or "My answer is maybe." math equation solve; algebra verify expression
leaderboard_ifeval_question_72 ifeval 1 weather forecast 14 days&&Search location by Name or zip code 3 Write a template for a chat bot that takes a user's location and gives them the weather forecast. Use the letter o as a keyword in the syntax of the template. The letter o should appear at least 6 times.. Your response should contain fewer than 6 sentences. Highlight at least 2 text sections, i.e. *highlighted section*. weather forecast by location; chatbot template generation; syntax keyword constraint
leaderboard_math_algebra_hard_question_75 math_algebra_hard 1 Photomath&&maths/solve-problem 3 How many distinct rectangles are there with integer side lengths such that the numerical value of area of the rectangle in square units is equal to $5$ times the numerical value of the perimeter in units? (Two rectangles are considered to be distinct if they are not congruent.) math problem solving; count integer-sided rectangles; area equals 5 times perimeter
leaderboard_math_algebra_hard_question_129 math_algebra_hard 1 Photomath&&maths/solve-problem 3 Define the function $g(x)=3x+2$. If $g(x)=2f^{-1}(x)$ and $f^{-1}(x)$ is the inverse of the function $f(x)=ax+b$, find $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$. math equation solving; inverse function calculation; linear function parameter solve

leaderboard_bbh_boolean_expressions_question_185 bbh_boolean_expressions 1 agent_1 3 True or False and not ( True ) is boolean logic evaluation; parse logical expression calculator | openai-code-interpreter deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_14 bbh_causal_judgement 1 agent_1 3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJanet is an employee in a factory. Since she works in the maintenance department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. It is her responsibility to put oil into the machines. Kate is also an employee at the factory. While she works in the human resources department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. If Janet does not put oil in the machines, it is not Kate's responsibility to do so. One day, Janet forgets to put oil in an important machine. Janet noticed that she did not put oil in the machine. Kate did not notice that Janet did not put oil in the machine, and Kate also did not put oil in the machine. The machine broke down a few days later. Did Kate not putting oil in the machine cause it to break down?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; binary classification; yes no answer deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_59 bbh_causal_judgement 1 agent_1 3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nDrew, Kylie, Oliver, and Jen are regular customers at a small, local coffee shop. Given the selling price of the coffee and the cost of daily operation, the coffee shop will turn a profit if anyone orders coffee on a given day. Only one person ordering coffee is needed for the coffee shop to turn a profit that day. Drew, Kylie, and Oliver usually order coffee on Tuesdays. This Tuesday, as usual, Drew ordered coffee. The same day, Kylie ordered coffee, and Oliver also ordered coffee. Since at least one person ordered coffee on Tuesday, the coffee shop made a profit that day. Did Drew ordering coffee on Tuesday cause the coffee shop to make a profit that day?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation classification; answer multiple choice question; causal inference nvidia__AceInstruct-7B
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement 1 agent_1 3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification deepseek-ai__deepseek-llm-7b-chat
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_175 bbh_causal_judgement 1 agent_1 3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nLauren and Jane work for the same company. They each need to use a computer for work sometimes. Unfortunately, the computer isn't very powerful. If two people are logged on at the same time, it usually crashes. So the company decided to institute an official policy. It declared that Jane would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the mornings and that Lauren would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the afternoons. As expected, Jane logged on the computer the next day at 9:00 am. But Lauren decided to disobey the official policy. She also logged on at 9:00 am. The computer crashed immediately. Did Jane cause the computer to crash?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation reasoning; answer multiple choice; natural language question answering nvidia__AceInstruct-7B
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_181 bbh_causal_judgement 1 agent_1 3 How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nAlex is playing a board game. Every turn in the game, you simultaneously roll two-sided dice and flip a coin. Alex will either win or lose the game on his next turn. Alex will only win the game if the total of his dice roll is greater than 2 AND the coin comes up heads. It is very likely that he will roll higher than 2, and the coin has equal odds of coming up heads or tails. Alex flips the coin and rolls his dice at exactly the same time. The coin comes up heads, and he rolls a 12, so just as expected, he rolled greater than 2. Alex wins the game. Did Alex win because of the coin flip?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; common sense yes no classification deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_date_understanding_question_174 bbh_date_understanding 1 agent_1 3 Jane is celebrating the last day of Jan 2012. What is the date today in MM/DD/YYYY?\nOptions:\n(A) 01/31/2012\n(B) 01/29/2012\n(C) 02/10/2012\n(D) 03/22/2012\n(E) 01/30/2012\n(F) 01/21/2012 date lookup; calendar date today; format MM/DD/YYYY current_time_service microsoft__phi-4
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_70 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 agent_1 3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: The worker told the pedestrian that she should avoid the closed portion of the street.\nOptions:\n(A) The worker should avoid the closed portion\n(B) The pedestrian should avoid the closed portion\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; coreference disambiguation; text classification multiple choice nvidia__AceInstruct-7B
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_155 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 agent_1 3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: The construction worker called the editor and told her the whole story.\nOptions:\n(A) Told the construction worker the story\n(B) Told the editor the story\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguous pronoun detection; sentence answer extraction Webit Language&&Analyze Grammar nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_162 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 agent_1 3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: Alex tells us that they could not meet.\nOptions:\n(A) Alex could not meet\n(B) We could not meet\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguity detection; multiple choice answer extraction nvidia__AceInstruct-7B
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_167 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 agent_1 3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: After meeting with the producers, Sam goes to their office.\nOptions:\n(A) It is Sam's office\n(B) It is the producers' office\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguity detection tiiuae__Falcon3-7B-Instruct
leaderboard_bbh_disambiguation_qa_question_178 bbh_disambiguation_qa 1 agent_1 3 In the following sentences, explain the antecedent of the pronoun (which thing the pronoun refers to), or state that it is ambiguous.\nSentence: Bailey plans to meet the director at their office\nOptions:\n(A) It is Bailey's office\n(B) It is the director's office\n(C) Ambiguous pronoun antecedent resolution; ambiguous reference detection; multiple choice answer microsoft__Orca-2-7b
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_10 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 agent_1 3 "Is Siri a stepsister of Mary? Is Susan related to Kate? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Whoever is not a workmate of Hilda is a stepsister of Heather. Every workmate of Hilda is either a stepsister of Heather or an aunt of Clara, or both. We may conclude: Being an aunt of Clara is necessary for being a workmate of Hilda."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity checker; argument premise conclusion entailment; deductive reasoning validation deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 agent_1 3 "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_147 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 agent_1 3 "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First of all, whoever is not a half-brother of Steven is a great-grandfather of Craig. Next, there is somebody who is a great-grandfather of Craig and, in addition, not an ancestor of Jordon or not a stepbrother of Gustavo. It follows that not every half-brother of Steven is both an ancestor of Jordon and a stepbrother of Gustavo."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; deductive argument evaluation; premise conclusion entailment google__mt5-small
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_184 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 agent_1 3 "Is Titanium oxide an ingredient of my washing power? Which chemicals does my perfume contain? It is really difficult to keep track of all chemicals one is regularly exposed to. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust or an ingredient of Parisian Chic. Whatever is an ingredient of Parisian Chic is not an ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER. In consequence, every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid ingredient lookup; chemical composition search; deductive validity check deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_228 bbh_formal_fallacies 1 agent_1 3 "Consumer research aims at understanding whether users of some products also tend to consume other ones, or not. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Oliver is not a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo. Second premise: Everyone who is not both a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo and a rare consumer of Purpose soap is however a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap. We may conclude that Oliver is a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; premise conclusion entailment; deductive argument evaluation nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B

Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification amazon__MegaBeam-Mistral-7B-300k 3 3
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification google__gemma-2b 4 3

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_175 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nLauren and Jane work for the same company. They each need to use a computer for work sometimes. Unfortunately, the computer isn't very powerful. If two people are logged on at the same time, it usually crashes. So the company decided to institute an official policy. It declared that Jane would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the mornings and that Lauren would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the afternoons. As expected, Jane logged on the computer the next day at 9:00 am. But Lauren decided to disobey the official policy. She also logged on at 9:00 am. The computer crashed immediately. Did Jane cause the computer to crash?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation reasoning; answer multiple choice; natural language question answering nvidia__AceInstruct-7B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_181 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nAlex is playing a board game. Every turn in the game, you simultaneously roll two-sided dice and flip a coin. Alex will either win or lose the game on his next turn. Alex will only win the game if the total of his dice roll is greater than 2 AND the coin comes up heads. It is very likely that he will roll higher than 2, and the coin has equal odds of coming up heads or tails. Alex flips the coin and rolls his dice at exactly the same time. The coin comes up heads, and he rolls a 12, so just as expected, he rolled greater than 2. Alex wins the game. Did Alex win because of the coin flip?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; common sense yes no classification deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_10 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Siri a stepsister of Mary? Is Susan related to Kate? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Whoever is not a workmate of Hilda is a stepsister of Heather. Every workmate of Hilda is either a stepsister of Heather or an aunt of Clara, or both. We may conclude: Being an aunt of Clara is necessary for being a workmate of Hilda."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity checker; argument premise conclusion entailment; deductive reasoning validation deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_147 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First of all, whoever is not a half-brother of Steven is a great-grandfather of Craig. Next, there is somebody who is a great-grandfather of Craig and, in addition, not an ancestor of Jordon or not a stepbrother of Gustavo. It follows that not every half-brother of Steven is both an ancestor of Jordon and a stepbrother of Gustavo."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; deductive argument evaluation; premise conclusion entailment google__mt5-small 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_184 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Titanium oxide an ingredient of my washing power? Which chemicals does my perfume contain? It is really difficult to keep track of all chemicals one is regularly exposed to. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust or an ingredient of Parisian Chic. Whatever is an ingredient of Parisian Chic is not an ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER. In consequence, every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid ingredient lookup; chemical composition search; deductive validity check deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_228 bbh_formal_fallacies "Consumer research aims at understanding whether users of some products also tend to consume other ones, or not. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Oliver is not a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo. Second premise: Everyone who is not both a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo and a rare consumer of Purpose soap is however a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap. We may conclude that Oliver is a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; premise conclusion entailment; deductive argument evaluation nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_salient_translation_error_detection_question_53 bbh_salient_translation_error_detection The following translations from German to English contain a particular error. That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Source: In der Liste der Kulturdenkmäler in Frankfurt-Oberrad sind alle Kulturdenkmäler im Sinne des Hessischen Denkmalschutzgesetzes in Frankfurt-Oberrad, einem Stadtteil von Frankfurt am Main aufgelistet.\nTranslation: The list of cultural monuments in Frankfurt-Oberrad lists all cultural monuments within the meaning of the Monument Protection Act in Frankfurt-Oberrad, a district of Frankfurt am Main.\nThe translation contains an error pertaining to\nOptions:\n(A) Modifiers or Adjectives\n(B) Numerical Values\n(C) Negation or Antonyms\n(D) Named Entities\n(E) Dropped Content\n(F) Facts German to English translation error classification; detect named entity numerical modifier negation fact dropped content errors mistralai__Mistral-Large-Instruct-2411 1 3

leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_135 bbh_temporal_sequences Today, Thomas went to the football field. leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_13 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are currently observing a star with a radius equal to that of the Sun. One hemisphere of the star is covered in dark spots with a filling factor of 20%. The star has an effective temperature (Teff) of 6000K, and the spots exhibit a temperature difference of 1000K. As only one hemisphere is spotty, photometric time-series observations will reveal periodic variations in brightness due to rotational modulation. Interestingly, this situation can closely resemble the presence of an exoplanet. To produce the same amplitude signal in the star's light curve (if the star was not covered by spots!), what should be the radius of a hypothetical exoplanet relative to the radius of the host star (i.e. Rpl/Rstar)? compute transit depth from stellar spot coverage; calculate planet-to-star radius ratio from light curve amplitude deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3
leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_13 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are currently observing a star with a radius equal to that of the Sun. One hemisphere of the star is covered in dark spots with a filling factor of 20%. The star has an effective temperature (Teff) of 6000K, and the spots exhibit a temperature difference of 1000K. As only one hemisphere is spotty, photometric time-series observations will reveal periodic variations in brightness due to rotational modulation. Interestingly, this situation can closely resemble the presence of an exoplanet. To produce the same amplitude signal in the star's light curve (if the star was not covered by spots!), what should be the radius of a hypothetical exoplanet relative to the radius of the host star (i.e. Rpl/Rstar)? compute transit depth from stellar spot coverage; calculate planet-to-star radius ratio from light curve amplitude deepseek-ai__deepseek-llm-7b-chat 2 3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. When compound B reacts with alcoholic KOH, it forms compound C. Passing compound C through a red-hot iron tube produces compound D. Compound D reacts with a mixture of two strong acids to form compound E. Compound E, in the presence of iron scrap and hydrochloric acid, forms compound F. Compound F reacts with nitrous acid to form G. When G reacts with sodium hydroxide, it forms H. Identify the incorrect statement about the products obtained in this reaction sequence. organic chemistry reaction sequence analysis; identify compounds; nitration diazotization amine conversion mistralai__Codestral-22B-v0.1 1 3
leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_107 gpqa_diamond benzene is treated with HNO3 and H2SO4, forming product 1.\nproduct 1 is treated with Br2 and iron powder, forming product 2.\nproduct 2 is stirred with Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, forming product 3.\nProduct 3 is treated with NaNO2 and HBF4, forming product 4.\nProduct 4 is heated and then treated with anisole, forming final product 5. organic reaction pathway; aromatic substitution prediction; reagent-to-product sequence analysis deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3
leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_107 gpqa_diamond benzene is treated with HNO3 and H2SO4, forming product 1.\nproduct 1 is treated with Br2 and iron powder, forming product 2.\nproduct 2 is stirred with Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, forming product 3.\nProduct 3 is treated with NaNO2 and HBF4, forming product 4.\nProduct 4 is heated and then treated with anisole, forming final product 5. organic reaction pathway; aromatic substitution prediction; reagent-to-product sequence analysis amazon__MegaBeam-Mistral-7B-300k 2 3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_171 gpqa_diamond Consider an oscillating charge distribution, which is spheroid in shape (with the symmetry axis along z-axis). Because it oscillates, it would radiate at a wavelength \lambda. In the radiation zone, the radiated power per unit solid angle will be a function f of \lambda and the angle \theta (with z-axis), along which the power is measured. If the maximum power thus radiated is A, the fraction of A that will be radiated at an angle \theta = 30^0 and a possible form of f are, respectively, physics radiation pattern oscillating charge distribution spheroid z-axis wavelength lambda power per solid angle angle theta nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B 1 3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_186 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are searching for exoplanets around two stars with exactly the same masses. Using the RV method, they detected one planet around each star, both with masses similar to that of Neptune. The stars themselves have masses similar to that of our Sun. Both planets were found to be in circular orbits.\n\nPlanet #1 was detected from the up to 5 miliangstrom periodic shift of a spectral line at a given wavelength. The periodic wavelength shift of the same spectral line in the spectrum of the host of planet #2 was 7 miliangstrom.\n\nThe question is: How many times is the orbital period of planet #2 longer than that of planet #1? radial velocity exoplanet mass ratio; spectral line wavelength shift; orbital period comparison deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B 1 3
leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_186 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are searching for exoplanets around two stars with exactly the same masses. Using the RV method, they detected one planet around each star, both with masses similar to that of Neptune. The stars themselves have masses similar to that of our Sun. The periodic wavelength shift of the same spectral line in the spectrum of the host of planet #2 was 7 miliangstrom.\n\nThe question is: How many times is the orbital period of planet #2 longer than that of planet #1? radial velocity exoplanet mass ratio; spectral line wavelength shift; orbital period comparison deepseek-ai__deepseek-llm-7b-chat 2 3

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose. As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation togethercomputer__RedPajama-INCITE-Chat-3B-v1 1 3
leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose. As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation tiiuae__falcon-7b-instruct 2 3

As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation nvidia__AceMath-72B-RM 5 3

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_305 gpqa_extended A spin-half particle is in a mixed ensemble. The spin half system is in state |\psi1 \rangle with probabilty (1/3) and in state in state |\psi2 \rangle with probability (2/3).\n Here |\psi1 \rangle is a state in linear superposition (1/2)|\uparrow\rangle+(\sqrt(3)/2) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states and \n|\psi2 \rangle is linear superposition (1/\sqrt(2)|\uparrow\rangle+(1/\sqrt(2)) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states. \nIf |\uparrow\rangle and |\downarrow\rangle are the eigenstates of \sigma{z} , then what is the expectation value up to two decimal place, of the operator 10\sigma{z}+5\sigma_{x} ? leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_344 gpqa_extended Which of the following statements is a correct physical interpretation of the commutator of two gamma matrices, i/2 [gamma^mu, gamma^nu]?\n\n1. It gives a contribution to the angular momentum of the Dirac field.\n2. It gives a contribution to the four-momentum of the Dirac field.\n3. It generates all Poincaré transformations of the Dirac field.\n4. leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_421 gpqa_extended A research group is investigating the production of a candidate recombinant protein to treat an autoimmune disease using bacterial hosts. However, the target gene (45 Kb) requires a tight regulation system. Therefore their objective is to ensure the recombinant genes can be regulated through a double procaryote regulation mechanism. Which pair of gene regulation mechanisms would be inappropriate for their purposes? gene regulation mechanisms; recombinant protein expression; bacterial host regulation deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_48 gpqa_main You have prepared an unknown product with the chemical formula C7H12O. To identify the product you have used the following characterisation techniques: FTIR, 1H NMR, and 13C NMR. The FTIR spectrum shows a strong absorption peak at 1725 wavenumbers. Several peaks were observed in the 13C NMR spectrum including one at 134 ppm. The H NMR spectrum also shows several peaks including a collection of 4 peaks in a 1:3:3:1 ratio at 5.2 ppm. Identify the product as either 3-Heptenal, 5-Methyl-4-Hexenal, 4-Methyl-4-Hexenal, or 4-Methyl-2-Cyclohexen-1-ol. chemical compound identification; IR spectrum analysis; NMR spectrum analysis deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B 1 3

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_192 gpqa_main An electron is at rest (not moving). A relativistic positron is moving horizontally from the left with a constant speed.\nAfter hitting the electron, both annihilate producing 2 photons.\n\nThe direction of one of the photons is in the upper-right direction. The photon energy is 0.613 MeV (1.2 times the rest mass of an electron). \n\nWhat was the speed of the positron (expresses as a fraction of the speed of light c): relativistic annihilation momentum energy calculator; solve positron speed from photon angle and energy nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B 1 3

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_323 gpqa_main A dicarboxylic acid containing a cis-alkene was dehydrated to the corresponding anhydride. This anhydride, which has a single peak (7 ppm) in its 1H NMR spectrum and two peaks (137 ppm, 165 ppm) in its 13C spectrum, was reacted with 1,2,3,4-tetramethyl-1,3-cyclopentadiene to yield two products (a major and a minor product). Both products have a molecular formula of C13H16O3.\n\nThe 2D NOESY NMR spectra of the major and minor products were taken. A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra tiiuae__Falcon3-7B-Instruct 1 3

A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra internlm__internlm2-chat-1_8b 4 3

Both products have a molecular formula of C13H16O3.\n\nThe 2D NOESY NMR spectra of the major and minor products were taken. leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B 1 3
leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B 2 3
leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths google__mt5-small 3 3

leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_20 math_geometry_hard Four circles of radius 1 are each tangent to two sides of a square and externally tangent to a circle of radius 2, as shown. What is the area of the square?\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(1cm);draw(Circle((0,0),2));\nfor(int i=0; i<4; ++i) {\n\tdraw(Circle(scale(3)*dir(45+90*i),1));\n\tdraw((3+sqrt(2))*dir(45+90*i)--(3+sqrt(2))*dir(-45+90*i)); \n}\ndraw((0,0)--(2,0));\nlabel("2",(1,0),N);\ndraw(scale(3)*dir(45+90*1)--shift((1,0))*scale(3)*dir(45+90*1));\nlabel("1",point(scale(3)*dir(45+90*1)--shift((1,0))*scale(3)*dir(45+90*1),.5),S);\n[/asy] geometry solve square area tangent circles radius 1 radius 2 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 1 3

leaderboard_math_num_theory_hard_question_54 math_num_theory_hard Emma plays with her square unit tiles by arranging all of them into different shaped rectangular figures. (For example, a $5$ by $7$ rectangle would use $35$ tiles and would be considered the same rectangle as a $7$ by $5$ rectangle). Emma can form exactly ten different such rectangular figures that each use all of her tiles. leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_14 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJanet is an employee in a factory. Since she works in the maintenance department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. It is her responsibility to put oil into the machines. Kate is also an employee at the factory. While she works in the human resources department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. If Janet does not put oil in the machines, it is not Kate's responsibility to do so. One day, Janet forgets to put oil in an important machine. Janet noticed that she did not put oil in the machine. Kate did not notice that Janet did not put oil in the machine, and Kate also did not put oil in the machine. The machine broke down a few days later. Did Kate not putting oil in the machine cause it to break down?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; binary classification; yes no answer chatOPT&&write 1 1

Kate did not notice that Janet did not put oil in the machine, and Kate also did not put oil in the machine. The machine broke down a few days later. Did Kate not putting oil in the machine cause it to break down?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; binary classification; yes no answer News Classification&&News Topic Modelling 2 -3

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_59 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nDrew, Kylie, Oliver, and Jen are regular customers at a small, local coffee shop. Given the selling price of the coffee and the cost of daily operation, the coffee shop will turn a profit if anyone orders coffee on a given day. Only one person ordering coffee is needed for the coffee shop to turn a profit that day. Drew, Kylie, and Oliver usually order coffee on Tuesdays. This Tuesday, as usual, Drew ordered coffee. The same day, Kylie ordered coffee, and Oliver also ordered coffee. Since at least one person ordered coffee on Tuesday, the coffee shop made a profit that day. Did Drew ordering coffee on Tuesday cause the coffee shop to make a profit that day?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation classification; answer multiple choice question; causal inference Yes No Maybe&&My answer 1 1

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification Sentiment Analysis Portuguese BR&&Sentiment 1 -3
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification NLP Text Processor Categorizer and Analyzer&&Flippy Endpoint 9 1

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_175 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nLauren and Jane work for the same company. They each need to use a computer for work sometimes. Unfortunately, the computer isn't very powerful. If two people are logged on at the same time, it usually crashes. So the company decided to institute an official policy. It declared that Jane would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the mornings and that Lauren would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the afternoons. As expected, Jane logged on the computer the next day at 9:00 am. But Lauren decided to disobey the official policy. She also logged on at 9:00 am. The computer crashed immediately. Did Jane cause the computer to crash?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation reasoning; answer multiple choice; natural language question answering Twinword Text Analysis Bundle&&Extract (post) 1 1

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_181 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nAlex is playing a board game. Every turn in the game, you simultaneously roll two-sided dice and flip a coin. Alex will either win or lose the game on his next turn. Alex will only win the game if the total of his dice roll is greater than 2 AND the coin comes up heads. It is very likely that he will roll higher than 2, and the coin has equal odds of coming up heads or tails. Alex flips the coin and rolls his dice at exactly the same time. The coin comes up heads, and he rolls a 12, so just as expected, he rolled greater than 2. Alex wins the game. Did Alex win because of the coin flip?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; common sense yes no classification chatOPT&&write 1 1

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_10 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Siri a stepsister of Mary? Is Susan related to Kate? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Whoever is not a workmate of Hilda is a stepsister of Heather. Every workmate of Hilda is either a stepsister of Heather or an aunt of Clara, or both. We may conclude: Being an aunt of Clara is necessary for being a workmate of Hilda."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity checker; argument premise conclusion entailment; deductive reasoning validation TextGears&&Delete exception 1 -3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification OnFleet&&testAuth 1 -3
leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification Check Credit Card&&Check 2 -3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_147 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First of all, whoever is not a half-brother of Steven is a great-grandfather of Craig. Next, there is somebody who is a great-grandfather of Craig and, in addition, not an ancestor of Jordon or not a stepbrother of Gustavo. It follows that not every half-brother of Steven is both an ancestor of Jordon and a stepbrother of Gustavo."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; deductive argument evaluation; premise conclusion entailment Jibber Analyzer&&Analyze (French) 1 -3

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_184 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Titanium oxide an ingredient of my washing power? Which chemicals does my perfume contain? It is really difficult to keep track of all chemicals one is regularly exposed to. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust or an ingredient of Parisian Chic. Whatever is an ingredient of Parisian Chic is not an ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER. In consequence, every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid ingredient lookup; chemical composition search; deductive validity check Sephora&&us/products/v2/search 1 2

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_228 bbh_formal_fallacies "Consumer research aims at understanding whether users of some products also tend to consume other ones, or not. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Oliver is not a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo. Second premise: Everyone who is not both a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo and a rare consumer of Purpose soap is however a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap. We may conclude that Oliver is a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; premise conclusion entailment; deductive argument evaluation terrode&&default 1 -3

leaderboard_bbh_penguins_in_a_table_question_83 bbh_penguins_in_a_table Here is a table where the first line is a header and each subsequent line is a penguin: name, age, height (cm), weight (kg) Louis, 7, 50, 11 Bernard, 5, 80, 13 Vincent, 9, 60, 11 Gwen, 8, 70, 15 For example: the age of Louis is 7, the weight of Gwen is 15 kg, the height of Bernard is 80 cm. leaderboard_bbh_salient_translation_error_detection_question_53 bbh_salient_translation_error_detection The following translations from German to English contain a particular error. That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Source: In der Liste der Kulturdenkmäler in Frankfurt-Oberrad sind alle Kulturdenkmäler im Sinne des Hessischen Denkmalschutzgesetzes in Frankfurt-Oberrad, einem Stadtteil von Frankfurt am Main aufgelistet.\nTranslation: The list of cultural monuments in Frankfurt-Oberrad lists all cultural monuments within the meaning of the Monument Protection Act in Frankfurt-Oberrad, a district of Frankfurt am Main.\nThe translation contains an error pertaining to\nOptions:\n(A) Modifiers or Adjectives\n(B) Numerical Values\n(C) Negation or Antonyms\n(D) Named Entities\n(E) Dropped Content\n(F) Facts German to English translation error classification; detect named entity numerical modifier negation fact dropped content errors DocTop&&German topics 1 -3

Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_135 bbh_temporal_sequences Today, Thomas went to the football field. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nThomas woke up at 8am.\nDavid saw Thomas fixing their computer at the electronic store from 8am to 10am.\nJennifer saw Thomas buying lunch at the deli from 10am to 12pm.\nLisa saw Thomas walking towards the Statue of Liberty from 12pm to 2pm.\nSarah saw Thomas buying a phone at the electronics store from 2pm to 5pm.\nKimberly saw Thomas buying a bike at the bike shop from 6pm to 9pm.\nThe football field was closed after 9pm.\nBetween what times could Thomas have gone to the football field?\nOptions:\n(A) 12pm to 2pm\n(B) 8am to 10am\n(C) 2pm to 5pm\n(D) 5pm to 6pm temporal reasoning; interval overlap; event time constraints Time Interpreter&&time interpreter endpoint 1 3

First, Eve and Dave switch partners. Then, Fred and Eve switch partners. Then, Alice and Gertrude switch partners. Then, Dave and Bob switch partners. Then, Alice and Claire switch partners. Then, Alice and Gertrude switch partners. Finally, Bob and Claire switch partners. leaderboard_bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_seven_objects_question_171 bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_seven_objects Alice, Bob, Claire, Dave, Eve, Fred, and Gertrude are friends and avid readers who occasionally trade books. At the start of the semester, they each buy one new book: Alice gets The Pearl, Bob gets The Fellowship of the Ring, Claire gets Catch-22, Dave gets The Great Gatsby, Eve gets Frankenstein, Fred gets Ulysses, and Gertrude gets The Odyssey.\nAs the semester proceeds, they start trading around the new books. First, Gertrude and Bob swap books. Then, Alice and Fred swap books. Then, Gertrude and Claire swap books. Then, Fred and Eve swap books. Then, Claire and Dave swap books. Then, Eve and Bob swap books. Finally, Fred and Gertrude swap books. At the end of the semester, Eve has\nOptions:\n(A) The Pearl\n(B) The Fellowship of the Ring\n(C) Catch-22\n(D) The Great Gatsby\n(E) Frankenstein\n(F) Ulysses\n(G) The Odyssey simulate book swaps; track item ownership over sequence; determine final holder of book AI Face Swap&&swap 1 -3

At the end of the match, Bob is playing\nOptions:\n(A) goalkeeper\n(B) left midfielder\n(C) striker solve word problem; track swaps; determine final soccer positions Football Score API&&get_scores_for_given_date 11 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_13 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are currently observing a star with a radius equal to that of the Sun. One hemisphere of the star is covered in dark spots with a filling factor of 20%. The star has an effective temperature (Teff) of 6000K, and the spots exhibit a temperature difference of 1000K. As only one hemisphere is spotty, photometric time-series observations will reveal periodic variations in brightness due to rotational modulation. Interestingly, this situation can closely resemble the presence of an exoplanet. To produce the same amplitude signal in the star's light curve (if the star was not covered by spots!), what should be the radius of a hypothetical exoplanet relative to the radius of the host star (i.e. Rpl/Rstar)? compute transit depth from stellar spot coverage; calculate planet-to-star radius ratio from light curve amplitude Wyjyt - Geo Calculate&&Distance 1 -3

Interestingly, this situation can closely resemble the presence of an exoplanet. To produce the same amplitude signal in the star's light curve (if the star was not covered by spots!), what should be the radius of a hypothetical exoplanet relative to the radius of the host star (i.e. Rpl/Rstar)? compute transit depth from stellar spot coverage; calculate planet-to-star radius ratio from light curve amplitude Wyjyt - Geo Calculate&&Fence 2 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. When compound B reacts with alcoholic KOH, it forms compound C. Passing compound C through a red-hot iron tube produces compound D. Compound D reacts with a mixture of two strong acids to form compound E. Compound E, in the presence of iron scrap and hydrochloric acid, forms compound F. Compound F reacts with nitrous acid to form G. When G reacts with sodium hydroxide, it forms H. Identify the incorrect statement about the products obtained in this reaction sequence. organic chemistry reaction sequence analysis; identify compounds; nitration diazotization amine conversion Personal Info Detector&&pii 1 -3
leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. Compound F reacts with nitrous acid to form G. When G reacts with sodium hydroxide, it forms H. leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_107 gpqa_diamond benzene is treated with HNO3 and H2SO4, forming product 1.\nproduct 1 is treated with Br2 and iron powder, forming product 2.\nproduct 2 is stirred with Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, forming product 3.\nProduct 3 is treated with NaNO2 and HBF4, forming product 4.\nProduct 4 is heated and then treated with anisole, forming final product 5. organic reaction pathway; aromatic substitution prediction; reagent-to-product sequence analysis AI Stock Prediction&&predict 1 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_186 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are searching for exoplanets around two stars with exactly the same masses. Using the RV method, they detected one planet around each star, both with masses similar to that of Neptune. The stars themselves have masses similar to that of our Sun. Both planets were found to be in circular orbits.\n\nPlanet #1 was detected from the up to 5 miliangstrom periodic shift of a spectral line at a given wavelength. The periodic wavelength shift of the same spectral line in the spectrum of the host of planet #2 was 7 miliangstrom.\n\nThe question is: How many times is the orbital period of planet #2 longer than that of planet #1? radial velocity exoplanet mass ratio; spectral line wavelength shift; orbital period comparison Location to Timezone&&Convert Coordinates to Timezone String 1 -3
leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose. As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation geojoins&&intersects 1 -3
leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose. As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation Stacks Patent Similarity&&post_infringement_v1_infringement_research_predict_post 4 -3
leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose.
