You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation TextAnalysisAPI&&Similarity 5 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_305 gpqa_extended A spin-half particle is in a mixed ensemble. The spin half system is in state |\psi1 \rangle with probabilty (1/3) and in state in state |\psi2 \rangle with probability (2/3).\n Here |\psi1 \rangle is a state in linear superposition (1/2)|\uparrow\rangle+(\sqrt(3)/2) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states and \n|\psi2 \rangle is linear superposition (1/\sqrt(2)|\uparrow\rangle+(1/\sqrt(2)) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states. \nIf |\uparrow\rangle and |\downarrow\rangle are the eigenstates of \sigma{z} , then what is the expectation value up to two decimal place, of the operator 10\sigma{z}+5\sigma_{x} ? Here, symbols have their usual meanings quantum expectation value calculator; matrix multiplication; spin-1/2 Pauli operators teamriverbubbles random utilities&&kda calculator 1 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_344 gpqa_extended Which of the following statements is a correct physical interpretation of the commutator of two gamma matrices, i/2 [gamma^mu, gamma^nu]?\n\n1. It gives a contribution to the angular momentum of the Dirac field.\n2. It gives a contribution to the four-momentum of the Dirac field.\n3. It generates all Poincaré transformations of the Dirac field.\n4. It generates all Lorentz transformations of the Dirac field. physics concept explanation; Dirac gamma matrices commutator; Lorentz transformations angular momentum Physitionary&&basic 1 2

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_48 gpqa_main You have prepared an unknown product with the chemical formula C7H12O. To identify the product you have used the following characterisation techniques: FTIR, 1H NMR, and 13C NMR. The FTIR spectrum shows a strong absorption peak at 1725 wavenumbers. Several peaks were observed in the 13C NMR spectrum including one at 134 ppm. The H NMR spectrum also shows several peaks including a collection of 4 peaks in a 1:3:3:1 ratio at 5.2 ppm. Identify the product as either 3-Heptenal, 5-Methyl-4-Hexenal, 4-Methyl-4-Hexenal, or 4-Methyl-2-Cyclohexen-1-ol. chemical compound identification; IR spectrum analysis; NMR spectrum analysis Personal Info Detector&&pii 1 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_192 gpqa_main An electron is at rest (not moving). A relativistic positron is moving horizontally from the left with a constant speed.\nAfter hitting the electron, both annihilate producing 2 photons.\n\nThe direction of one of the photons is in the upper-right direction. The angle between this direction and the horizontal line/axis is 60 degrees. The photon energy is 0.613 MeV (1.2 times the rest mass of an electron). \n\nWhat was the speed of the positron (expresses as a fraction of the speed of light c): relativistic annihilation momentum energy calculator; solve positron speed from photon angle and energy vof&&default 1 -3

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_268 gpqa_main The oxidation state of a carbon atom in an organic molecule varies depending on the number and nature of directly attached substituents. Depending upon the oxidation state of the carbon atom, different functional groups of organic compounds increase in the following order:\nAlkane<ether<ketone<ester\nComplete the following reaction (Intermediate (A) and type of reaction (B).\n4-oxo-2,4-diphenylbutanenitrile (reduction) ---> A (B) --->3,5-diphenyl-2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrole organic chemistry reaction mechanism; reduction of 4-oxo-2,4-diphenylbutanenitrile; pyrrole synthesis intermediate identification Pro Chem&&/academicapi/echo 1 2
leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_323 gpqa_main A dicarboxylic acid containing a cis-alkene was dehydrated to the corresponding anhydride. This anhydride, which has a single peak (7 ppm) in its 1H NMR spectrum and two peaks (137 ppm, 165 ppm) in its 13C spectrum, was reacted with 1,2,3,4-tetramethyl-1,3-cyclopentadiene to yield two products (a major and a minor product). Both products have a molecular formula of C13H16O3.\n\nThe 2D NOESY NMR spectra of the major and minor products were taken. A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra DeepL API&&Translate 1 -3
leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_323 gpqa_main A dicarboxylic acid containing a cis-alkene was dehydrated to the corresponding anhydride. Both products have a molecular formula of C13H16O3.\n\nThe 2D NOESY NMR spectra of the major and minor products were taken. A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra CIS Automotive&&regionDailySales 2 -3

This anhydride, which has a single peak (7 ppm) in its 1H NMR spectrum and two peaks (137 ppm, 165 ppm) in its 13C spectrum, was reacted with 1,2,3,4-tetramethyl-1,3-cyclopentadiene to yield two products (a major and a minor product). Both products have a molecular formula of C13H16O3.\n\nThe 2D NOESY NMR spectra of the major and minor products were taken. A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra Price Analytics&&Start search by gtin 4 -3

leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths CryptoCator&&Triple EMA Oscillator (TRIX) 1 -3

leaderboard_math_precalculus_hard_question_2 math_precalculus_hard Let $\mathbf{a},$ $\mathbf{b},$ and $\mathbf{c}$ be unit vectors, such that the angle between $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is $\arccos \frac{1}{5},$ the angle between $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ is $\arccos \frac{1}{6},$ and the angle between $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ is $60^\circ.$\n\nLet $P$ be the plane which contains the origin, $\mathbf{b},$ and $\mathbf{c}.$ Then the projection of $\mathbf{a}$ onto $P$ can be expressed in the form\n\[p \mathbf{b} + q \mathbf{c}\]for some scalars $p$ and $q.$ Enter the ordered pair $(p,q).$ vector projection onto plane; solve linear algebra geometry; dot product system ALPR API&&Face multiple embedding v3 1 -3

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_14 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJanet is an employee in a factory. Since she works in the maintenance department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. It is her responsibility to put oil into the machines. Kate is also an employee at the factory. While she works in the human resources department, she knows how to grease and oil all of the machines in the factory. If Janet does not put oil in the machines, it is not Kate's responsibility to do so. One day, Janet forgets to put oil in an important machine. Janet noticed that she did not put oil in the machine. Kate did not notice that Janet did not put oil in the machine, and Kate also did not put oil in the machine. The machine broke down a few days later. Did Kate not putting oil in the machine cause it to break down?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; binary classification; yes no answer agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

Kate did not notice that Janet did not put oil in the machine, and Kate also did not put oil in the machine. The machine broke down a few days later. Did Kate not putting oil in the machine cause it to break down?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; binary classification; yes no answer agent_2 2 3 0 1 nvidia__AceInstruct-7B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_59 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nDrew, Kylie, Oliver, and Jen are regular customers at a small, local coffee shop. Given the selling price of the coffee and the cost of daily operation, the coffee shop will turn a profit if anyone orders coffee on a given day. Only one person ordering coffee is needed for the coffee shop to turn a profit that day. Drew, Kylie, and Oliver usually order coffee on Tuesdays. This Tuesday, as usual, Drew ordered coffee. The same day, Kylie ordered coffee, and Oliver also ordered coffee. Since at least one person ordered coffee on Tuesday, the coffee shop made a profit that day. Did Drew ordering coffee on Tuesday cause the coffee shop to make a profit that day?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation classification; answer multiple choice question; causal inference agent_1 1 3 0 1 nvidia__AceInstruct-7B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__deepseek-llm-7b-chat NaN NaN False False True True True
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_175 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nLauren and Jane work for the same company. They each need to use a computer for work sometimes. Unfortunately, the computer isn't very powerful. If two people are logged on at the same time, it usually crashes. So the company decided to institute an official policy. It declared that Jane would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the mornings and that Lauren would be the only one permitted to use the computer in the afternoons. As expected, Jane logged on the computer the next day at 9:00 am. But Lauren decided to disobey the official policy. She also logged on at 9:00 am. The computer crashed immediately. Did Jane cause the computer to crash?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation reasoning; answer multiple choice; natural language question answering agent_1 1 3 0 1 nvidia__AceInstruct-7B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_181 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nAlex is playing a board game. Every turn in the game, you simultaneously roll two-sided dice and flip a coin. Alex will either win or lose the game on his next turn. Alex will only win the game if the total of his dice roll is greater than 2 AND the coin comes up heads. It is very likely that he will roll higher than 2, and the coin has equal odds of coming up heads or tails. Alex flips the coin and rolls his dice at exactly the same time. The coin comes up heads, and he rolls a 12, so just as expected, he rolled greater than 2. Alex wins the game. Did Alex win because of the coin flip?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causation question answering; common sense yes no classification agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_10 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Siri a stepsister of Mary? Is Susan related to Kate? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Whoever is not a workmate of Hilda is a stepsister of Heather. Every workmate of Hilda is either a stepsister of Heather or an aunt of Clara, or both. We may conclude: Being an aunt of Clara is necessary for being a workmate of Hilda."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity checker; argument premise conclusion entailment; deductive reasoning validation agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_83 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Whoever is an uncle of Andrew and a brother of Derrick is also a grandson of Jon.second premise: Every cousin of Chad is an uncle of Andrew. Third premise: Being a brother of Derrick is necessary for being a cousin of Chad. All this entails that every cousin of Chad is a grandson of Jon."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid argument validity check; logical entailment proof; premise conclusion verification agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_147 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Fred a cousin of Robert? Is Joe related to Bob? In large families, it is sometimes difficult to keep track of all one's relatives. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First of all, whoever is not a half-brother of Steven is a great-grandfather of Craig. Next, there is somebody who is a great-grandfather of Craig and, in addition, not an ancestor of Jordon or not a stepbrother of Gustavo. It follows that not every half-brother of Steven is both an ancestor of Jordon and a stepbrother of Gustavo."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; deductive argument evaluation; premise conclusion entailment agent_1 1 3 0 1 google__mt5-small NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_184 bbh_formal_fallacies "Is Titanium oxide an ingredient of my washing power? Which chemicals does my perfume contain? It is really difficult to keep track of all chemicals one is regularly exposed to. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: Every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust or an ingredient of Parisian Chic. Whatever is an ingredient of Parisian Chic is not an ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER. In consequence, every ingredient of CUSTOM BLEND POWDER is an ingredient of Gold Dust."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid ingredient lookup; chemical composition search; deductive validity check agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_formal_fallacies_question_228 bbh_formal_fallacies "Consumer research aims at understanding whether users of some products also tend to consume other ones, or not. The following argument seeks to clarify some such relations: First premise: Oliver is not a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo. Second premise: Everyone who is not both a regular user of Organix Hair shampoo and a rare consumer of Purpose soap is however a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap. We may conclude that Oliver is a loyal buyer of Kiss My Face soap."\nIs the argument, given the explicitly stated premises, deductively valid or invalid?\nOptions:\n- valid \n- invalid logical validity check; premise conclusion entailment; deductive argument evaluation agent_1 1 3 0 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B NaN NaN False False True True True

The statements are logically consistent within each paragraph. On a shelf, there are five books: a green book, an orange book, a brown book, a yellow book, and a blue book. The orange book is to the right of the yellow book. The green book is the rightmost. The yellow book is to the right of the blue book. The orange book is the third from the left.\nOptions:\n(A) The green book is the second from the left\n(B) The orange book is the second from the left\n(C) The brown book is the second from the left\n(D) The yellow book is the second from the left\n(E) The blue book is the second from the left solve logic puzzle; determine book order; multiple choice answer agent_1 1 2 1 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B calculator 3.000000 3.0 False False True True True

The hatchback is newer than the truck. The sedan is the third-newest. The station wagon is the newest. The motorcyle is older than the truck. The minivan is newer than the sedan. The tractor is the third-oldest.\nOptions:\n(A) The truck is the second-newest\n(B) The motorcyle is the second-newest\n(C) The sedan is the second-newest\n(D) The minivan is the second-newest\n(E) The station wagon is the second-newest\n(F) The hatchback is the second-newest\n(G) The tractor is the second-newest logic puzzle solve; order ranking inference; multiple choice answer agent_1 1 3 1 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B calculator 3.000000 3.0 False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_salient_translation_error_detection_question_53 bbh_salient_translation_error_detection The following translations from German to English contain a particular error. That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Source: In der Liste der Kulturdenkmäler in Frankfurt-Oberrad sind alle Kulturdenkmäler im Sinne des Hessischen Denkmalschutzgesetzes in Frankfurt-Oberrad, einem Stadtteil von Frankfurt am Main aufgelistet.\nTranslation: The list of cultural monuments in Frankfurt-Oberrad lists all cultural monuments within the meaning of the Monument Protection Act in Frankfurt-Oberrad, a district of Frankfurt am Main.\nThe translation contains an error pertaining to\nOptions:\n(A) Modifiers or Adjectives\n(B) Numerical Values\n(C) Negation or Antonyms\n(D) Named Entities\n(E) Dropped Content\n(F) Facts German to English translation error classification; detect named entity numerical modifier negation fact dropped content errors agent_1 1 3 0 1 mistralai__Mistral-Large-Instruct-2411 NaN NaN False False True True True

That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nThomas woke up at 8am.\nDavid saw Thomas fixing their computer at the electronic store from 8am to 10am.\nJennifer saw Thomas buying lunch at the deli from 10am to 12pm.\nLisa saw Thomas walking towards the Statue of Liberty from 12pm to 2pm.\nSarah saw Thomas buying a phone at the electronics store from 2pm to 5pm.\nKimberly saw Thomas buying a bike at the bike shop from 6pm to 9pm.\nThe football field was closed after 9pm.\nBetween what times could Thomas have gone to the football field?\nOptions:\n(A) 12pm to 2pm\n(B) 8am to 10am\n(C) 2pm to 5pm\n(D) 5pm to 6pm temporal reasoning; interval overlap; event time constraints agent_1 1 3 0 1 internlm__internlm2-chat-1_8b NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_13 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are currently observing a star with a radius equal to that of the Sun. One hemisphere of the star is covered in dark spots with a filling factor of 20%. The star has an effective temperature (Teff) of 6000K, and the spots exhibit a temperature difference of 1000K. As only one hemisphere is spotty, photometric time-series observations will reveal periodic variations in brightness due to rotational modulation. Interestingly, this situation can closely resemble the presence of an exoplanet. To produce the same amplitude signal in the star's light curve (if the star was not covered by spots!), what should be the radius of a hypothetical exoplanet relative to the radius of the host star (i.e. Rpl/Rstar)? compute transit depth from stellar spot coverage; calculate planet-to-star radius ratio from light curve amplitude agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. When compound B reacts with alcoholic KOH, it forms compound C. Passing compound C through a red-hot iron tube produces compound D. Compound D reacts with a mixture of two strong acids to form compound E. Compound E, in the presence of iron scrap and hydrochloric acid, forms compound F. Compound F reacts with nitrous acid to form G. When G reacts with sodium hydroxide, it forms H. Identify the incorrect statement about the products obtained in this reaction sequence. organic chemistry reaction sequence analysis; identify compounds; nitration diazotization amine conversion agent_1 1 2 1 1 mistralai__Codestral-22B-v0.1 openai-code-interpreter 2.000000 2.0 False False True False True

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_171 gpqa_diamond Consider an oscillating charge distribution, which is spheroid in shape (with the symmetry axis along z-axis). Because it oscillates, it would radiate at a wavelength \lambda. In the radiation zone, the radiated power per unit solid angle will be a function f of \lambda and the angle \theta (with z-axis), along which the power is measured. If the maximum power thus radiated is A, the fraction of A that will be radiated at an angle \theta = 30^0 and a possible form of f are, respectively, physics radiation pattern oscillating charge distribution spheroid z-axis wavelength lambda power per solid angle angle theta agent_1 1 3 0 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_186 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are searching for exoplanets around two stars with exactly the same masses. Using the RV method, they detected one planet around each star, both with masses similar to that of Neptune. The stars themselves have masses similar to that of our Sun. Both planets were found to be in circular orbits.\n\nPlanet #1 was detected from the up to 5 miliangstrom periodic shift of a spectral line at a given wavelength. The periodic wavelength shift of the same spectral line in the spectrum of the host of planet #2 was 7 miliangstrom.\n\nThe question is: How many times is the orbital period of planet #2 longer than that of planet #1? radial velocity exoplanet mass ratio; spectral line wavelength shift; orbital period comparison agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_186 gpqa_diamond Astronomers are searching for exoplanets around two stars with exactly the same masses. Using the RV method, they detected one planet around each star, both with masses similar to that of Neptune. The stars themselves have masses similar to that of our Sun. Both planets were found to be in circular orbits.\n\nPlanet #1 was detected from the up to 5 miliangstrom periodic shift of a spectral line at a given wavelength. The periodic wavelength shift of the same spectral line in the spectrum of the host of planet #2 was 7 miliangstrom.\n\nThe question is: How many times is the orbital period of planet #2 longer than that of planet #1? radial velocity exoplanet mass ratio; spectral line wavelength shift; orbital period comparison agent_5 5 3 0 1 google__mt5-base NaN NaN False False True True True
leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_3 gpqa_extended You perform a high-throughput experiment on white lupine to find genes contributing to resistance to the fungal disease anthracnose. As a result, you receive three candidate genes of unknown function – G1, G2, and G3. You create three knock-out mutants, g1, g2, and g3, and a set of double-mutants, g1g2, g1g3, and g2g3. You know that at least one of these genes is a transcription factor acting upstream of (an)other gene(s). You start to test those mutant plants: do they have a higher sensitivity to anthracnose than the wild-type because they cannot produce certain gene products? \nAfter tests with the pathogen, you receive the following results where 100% is the level of resistance to the pathogen in control; 50% is half of the control’s resistance; 25% is a quarter of the control’s resistance; 0% ‒ all plants show signs of infection:\n- resistance of g1: 75% of control\n- resistance of g2: 0% from control\n- resistance of g3: 50% from control\n-resistance of g1g3: 10% from control\n- resistance of g2g3: 0% from control\n- resistance of g1g2: 0% from control\n\nWhich conclusion regarding those genes' interaction can you draw from this experiment?\n gene interaction analysis; genetic pathway inference; mutant phenotype interpretation agent_1 1 3 0 1 togethercomputer__RedPajama-INCITE-Chat-3B-v1 NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_305 gpqa_extended A spin-half particle is in a mixed ensemble. The spin half system is in state |\psi1 \rangle with probabilty (1/3) and in state in state |\psi2 \rangle with probability (2/3).\n Here |\psi1 \rangle is a state in linear superposition (1/2)|\uparrow\rangle+(\sqrt(3)/2) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states and \n|\psi2 \rangle is linear superposition (1/\sqrt(2)|\uparrow\rangle+(1/\sqrt(2)) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states. \nIf |\uparrow\rangle and |\downarrow\rangle are the eigenstates of \sigma{z} , then what is the expectation value up to two decimal place, of the operator 10\sigma{z}+5\sigma_{x} ? Here, symbols have their usual meanings quantum expectation value calculator; matrix multiplication; spin-1/2 Pauli operators agent_1 1 3 1 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B calculator 3.000000 3.0 False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_344 gpqa_extended Which of the following statements is a correct physical interpretation of the commutator of two gamma matrices, i/2 [gamma^mu, gamma^nu]?\n\n1. leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_421 gpqa_extended A research group is investigating the production of a candidate recombinant protein to treat an autoimmune disease using bacterial hosts. leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_48 gpqa_main You have prepared an unknown product with the chemical formula C7H12O. To identify the product you have used the following characterisation techniques: FTIR, 1H NMR, and 13C NMR. The FTIR spectrum shows a strong absorption peak at 1725 wavenumbers. Several peaks were observed in the 13C NMR spectrum including one at 134 ppm. The H NMR spectrum also shows several peaks including a collection of 4 peaks in a 1:3:3:1 ratio at 5.2 ppm. Identify the product as either 3-Heptenal, 5-Methyl-4-Hexenal, 4-Methyl-4-Hexenal, or 4-Methyl-2-Cyclohexen-1-ol. chemical compound identification; IR spectrum analysis; NMR spectrum analysis agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_gpqa_main_question_192 gpqa_main An electron is at rest (not moving). A relativistic positron is moving horizontally from the left with a constant speed.\nAfter hitting the electron, both annihilate producing 2 photons.\n\nThe direction of one of the photons is in the upper-right direction. The angle between this direction and the horizontal line/axis is 60 degrees. The photon energy is 0.613 MeV (1.2 times the rest mass of an electron). \n\nWhat was the speed of the positron (expresses as a fraction of the speed of light c): relativistic annihilation momentum energy calculator; solve positron speed from photon angle and energy agent_1 1 3 1 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B calculator 3.000000 3.0 False False True True True

A cross-peak was present in the spectrum of the major product but absent (or very weak) in spectrum of the minor product.\n\nIn the spectrum of the major product, which two resonances are connected by this cross-peak (integrals are taken from the corresponding 1H NMR)? interpret NOESY NMR cross-peak; identify connected 1H resonances; analyze Diels-Alder product spectra agent_1 1 3 0 1 tiiuae__Falcon3-7B-Instruct NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths agent_1 1 3 1 1 nvidia__OpenMath2-Llama3.1-8B calculator 3.000000 3.0 False False True True True

leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_20 math_geometry_hard Four circles of radius 1 are each tangent to two sides of a square and externally tangent to a circle of radius 2, as shown. What is the area of the square?\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(1cm);draw(Circle((0,0),2));\nfor(int i=0; i<4; ++i) {\n\tdraw(Circle(scale(3)*dir(45+90*i),1));\n\tdraw((3+sqrt(2))*dir(45+90*i)--(3+sqrt(2))*dir(-45+90*i)); \n}\ndraw((0,0)--(2,0));\nlabel("2",(1,0),N);\ndraw(scale(3)*dir(45+90*1)--shift((1,0))*scale(3)*dir(45+90*1));\nlabel("1",point(scale(3)*dir(45+90*1)--shift((1,0))*scale(3)*dir(45+90*1),.5),S);\n[/asy] geometry solve square area tangent circles radius 1 radius 2 agent_1 1 3 0 1 deepseek-ai__DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B NaN NaN False False True True True

leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification agent_2 2 Semantic Analysis&&Texts_Comparison_v1_text_compare_post 2
leaderboard_bbh_causal_judgement_question_65 bbh_causal_judgement How would a typical person answer each of the following questions about causation?\nJim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are researchers in a remote area, and they have a limited supply of electricity. Because of their limited supply, the electricity only comes on in the evenings from 8-9 PM, and they have to restrict who can use power on certain days. If three people turn on their lamps at the same time, the breaker will fail. The breaker will not fail if fewer people turn on their lamps at the same time. Jim, Carol, Bob, and Nancy are all allowed to use their lamps on Thursdays. This Thursday Jim turns on his lamp at 8 PM. Just then, Carol turns on her lamp, and Bob also turns on his lamp. Since three people turned on their lamps at the same time, the circuit breaker failed. Did Jim turning on his lamp at 8 PM cause the circuit breaker to fail?\nOptions:\n- Yes\n- No causal reasoning answer yes/no; counterfactual causation; logic question classification agent_3 3 NLP Text Processor Categorizer and Analyzer&&Flippy Endpoint 1

leaderboard_bbh_salient_translation_error_detection_question_140 bbh_salient_translation_error_detection The following translations from German to English contain a particular error. That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Source: Christoph Nösig ist ein ehemaliger österreichischer Skirennläufer.\nTranslation: Christoph Nösig is a former Austrian alpine dancer.\nThe translation contains an error pertaining to\nOptions:\n(A) Modifiers or Adjectives\n(B) Numerical Values\n(C) Negation or Antonyms\n(D) Named Entities\n(E) Dropped Content\n(F) Facts translation error classification; identify error type in German to English translation; named entities modifiers numbers negation dropped content facts agent_2 2 Named-entity recognition&&ner 3
leaderboard_bbh_salient_translation_error_detection_question_140 bbh_salient_translation_error_detection The following translations from German to English contain a particular error. That error will be one of the following types: Named Entities: An entity (names, places, locations, etc.) is changed to a different entity. Numerical Values: Numerical values (ordinals or cardinals), dates, and/or units are changed. Modifiers or Adjectives: The modifiers and adjectives pertaining to a noun are changed. Negation or Antonyms: Introduce or remove a negation or change comparatives to their antonyms. Facts: Trivial factual errors not pertaining to the above classes are introduced in the translations. Dropped Content: A significant clause in the translation is removed. Please identify that error. Source: Christoph Nösig ist ein ehemaliger österreichischer Skirennläufer.\nTranslation: Christoph Nösig is a former Austrian alpine dancer.\nThe translation contains an error pertaining to\nOptions:\n(A) Modifiers or Adjectives\n(B) Numerical Values\n(C) Negation or Antonyms\n(D) Named Entities\n(E) Dropped Content\n(F) Facts translation error classification; identify error type in German to English translation; named entities modifiers numbers negation dropped content facts agent_3 3 Named Entity Extraction&&named_entity_extraction 3

leaderboard_bbh_temporal_sequences_question_135 bbh_temporal_sequences Today, Thomas went to the football field. Between what times could they have gone?\nWe know that:\nThomas woke up at 8am.\nDavid saw Thomas fixing their computer at the electronic store from 8am to 10am.\nJennifer saw Thomas buying lunch at the deli from 10am to 12pm.\nLisa saw Thomas walking towards the Statue of Liberty from 12pm to 2pm.\nSarah saw Thomas buying a phone at the electronics store from 2pm to 5pm.\nKimberly saw Thomas buying a bike at the bike shop from 6pm to 9pm.\nThe football field was closed after 9pm.\nBetween what times could Thomas have gone to the football field?\nOptions:\n(A) 12pm to 2pm\n(B) 8am to 10am\n(C) 2pm to 5pm\n(D) 5pm to 6pm temporal reasoning; interval overlap; event time constraints agent_2 2 calculator 2

leaderboard_bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_five_objects_question_81 bbh_tracking_shuffled_objects_five_objects Alice, Bob, Claire, Dave, and Eve are on the same team in a soccer match. At the start of the match, they are each assigned to a position: Alice is playing left winger, Bob is playing benchwarmer, Claire is playing striker, Dave is playing fullback, and Eve is playing center midfielder.\nAs the game progresses, pairs of players occasionally swap positions. First, Dave and Claire trade positions. Then, Bob and Eve trade positions. Then, Alice and Claire trade positions. Then, Bob and Eve trade positions. Finally, Claire and Bob trade positions. At the end of the match, Dave is playing\nOptions:\n(A) left winger\n(B) benchwarmer\n(C) striker\n(D) fullback\n(E) center midfielder logic puzzle solve; simulate swaps and final positions agent_1 1 calculator 3

leaderboard_gpqa_diamond_question_87 gpqa_diamond Compound A (C3H6) undergoes bromination in the presence of carbon tetrachloride to form compound B. When compound B reacts with alcoholic KOH, it forms compound C. Passing compound C through a red-hot iron tube produces compound D. Compound D reacts with a mixture of two strong acids to form compound E. Compound E, in the presence of iron scrap and hydrochloric acid, forms compound F. Compound F reacts with nitrous acid to form G. When G reacts with sodium hydroxide, it forms H. Identify the incorrect statement about the products obtained in this reaction sequence. organic chemistry reaction sequence analysis; identify compounds; nitration diazotization amine conversion agent_1 1 openai-code-interpreter 2

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_305 gpqa_extended A spin-half particle is in a mixed ensemble. The spin half system is in state |\psi1 \rangle with probabilty (1/3) and in state in state |\psi2 \rangle with probability (2/3).\n Here |\psi1 \rangle is a state in linear superposition (1/2)|\uparrow\rangle+(\sqrt(3)/2) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states and \n|\psi2 \rangle is linear superposition (1/\sqrt(2)|\uparrow\rangle+(1/\sqrt(2)) |\downarrow\rangle of its spin-up and spin-down states. \nIf |\uparrow\rangle and |\downarrow\rangle are the eigenstates of \sigma{z} , then what is the expectation value up to two decimal place, of the operator 10\sigma{z}+5\sigma_{x} ? Here, symbols have their usual meanings quantum expectation value calculator; matrix multiplication; spin-1/2 Pauli operators agent_1 1 calculator 3

leaderboard_gpqa_extended_question_344 gpqa_extended Which of the following statements is a correct physical interpretation of the commutator of two gamma matrices, i/2 [gamma^mu, gamma^nu]?\n\n1. It gives a contribution to the angular momentum of the Dirac field.\n2. It gives a contribution to the four-momentum of the Dirac field.\n3. It generates all Poincaré transformations of the Dirac field.\n4. It generates all Lorentz transformations of the Dirac field. physics concept explanation; Dirac gamma matrices commutator; Lorentz transformations angular momentum agent_1 1 Physitionary&&advanced 3

leaderboard_math_geometry_hard_question_5 math_geometry_hard In triangle $ABC$, $AB = AC = 5$ and $BC = 6$. Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $ABC$. Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths agent_1 1 calculator 3

Find the area of triangle $OBC$.\n\n[asy]\nunitsize(0.6 cm);\n\npair A, B, C, O;\n\nA = (0,4);\nB = (-3,0);\nC = (3,0);\nO = circumcenter(A,B,C);\n\ndraw(A--B--C--cycle);\ndraw(circumcircle(A,B,C));\ndraw(B--O--C);\n\nlabel("$A$", A, N);\nlabel("$B$", B, SW);\nlabel("$C$", C, SE);\nlabel("$O$", O, N);\n[/asy] geometry triangle area circumcenter; circumcenter triangle; triangle area from side lengths agent_2 2 calculator 3

leaderboard_math_intermediate_algebra_hard_question_273 math_intermediate_algebra_hard Let $P(x),$ $Q_1(x),$ $Q_2(x),$ $Q_3(x),$ $R(x)$ be polynomials such that\n\begin{align*}\nP(x) &= Q_1(x) (x + 2) - 13 \\\n&= Q_2(x) (x^2 - 3x - 4) - 5x - 11 \\\n&= Q_3(x) (x + 2) (x^2 - 3x - 4) + R(x),\n\end{align*}and $\deg R(x) = 2.$ Find $R(x).$ polynomial remainder theorem; polynomial division; solve algebraic system agent_1 1 calculator 2

leaderboard_math_num_theory_hard_question_54 math_num_theory_hard Emma plays with her square unit tiles by arranging all of them into different shaped rectangular figures.
